changeReason: Improved translation from Japanese. Old: "What Kind of Name Is Rena?"

TextENGNew: ``...Rena isn’t here. Do you know where she went, Keiichi?``
``Huh? She was right there just a moment ago...@k@vS20/01/440100027. Hey, Mion.@k@vS20/01/440100028. Where did Rena go?``
``Rena~? She probably went to the restroom.@k@vS20/03/440300024. I won’t stop you if you want to peek, but I will be calling the cops~!``
Nobody asked about that!
``...Satoko, do you know where Rena is?``
``Rena-san? I just passed her in the hallway a moment ago.@k@vS20/04/440400012. She’s on class duty today, so she had to go water the flower beds.``
``Ah, so it’s Rena’s turn for class duty. What a trooper.``
changeReason: Old says 'Sounds like a drag' (expressing task is unpleasant) while new says 'What a trooper' (acknowledging effort); the Japanese お疲れさまなことで is an expression of acknowledgment/sympathy for someone's work, not a complaint.

TextENGNew: ...Rena, Rena, Rena.
...Her name kept coming up, and a question suddenly occurred to me.
It’s rude to say this about someone’s name, but... it’s an unusual one, isn’t it?@kIt sounds like a foreigner’s name.
``I wonder what kind of name "Rena" is... What kanji does it use?``
``...Rena is just a nickname. She has a proper given name.``
``Wait, really?! I was sure that Rena Ryuugu was her real name.``
``Well, I suppose that’s understandable, since we only ever call her Rena-san.``
She even writes her name as ``@bレナ.@<Rena@>.``@kAt school, it might as well be her real name.
``I wonder what her real name is... Maybe I’ll ask her when she comes back!``
Satoko and Rika-chan exchanged a glance.
``...You don’t need to wait for her. We can tell you ourselves.``
``The “rei” from “courtesy” and the “na” from “Nara.” Her real name is Reina Ryuugu!``
``Reina, huh? ...Hmm.@k@vS20/01/440100033. So it’s read as Rena instead of Reina?@k@vS20/01/440100034. That’s a pretty interesting reading.``
``...No, that’s not quite right. Reina is the correct reading.``
``Rena-san told us herself that she wanted to be called Rena.@k@vS20/04/440400016. So that’s why we call her Rena-san.``
``Kei-chan... Rena is Rena, okay?@k@vS20/03/440300027. Only strangers call her Reina.@k@vS20/03/440300028. You understand what I’m saying, right?``
I understood what Mion meant.
It didn’t matter what her legal name was—the names we used among ourselves were all that mattered!
Rena Ryuugu is Rena. She’s no one else.
``So I was thinking—if I nominated myself a nickname, would you all start calling me by it?``
``If it’s a good one, sure. So what do you want to be called?``
``Echigoya.``
changeReason: Old translates 越後屋 as 'Con man' while new preserves the cultural reference 'Echigoya' (the stock corrupt merchant from period dramas); this changes the reader's understanding from a generic insult to a specific cultural role-play reference.

TextENGNew: Before long, Rena returned to the classroom.
A younger student near the entrance was telling Rena that people had been looking for her.
``Oh? Someone was looking for me, I wonder?@k@vS20/02/440200035. I wonder?``
Seeing that, Mion and I both grinned.
changeReason: Old says 'sneered' (contemptuous) while new says 'grinned' (playful satisfaction); the Japanese にんまりと笑う means to grin slyly/knowingly, which matches the playful mood rather than contempt.

TextENGNew: ``My lord, the Ryuugu woman has shown herself!!``
changeReason: Old says 'Bailiff' (wrong role) while new says 'My lord' (addressing the magistrate); in the Jidaigeki parody, Keiichi plays 越後屋 (the merchant) addressing the お代官様 (corrupt magistrate played by Mion).

TextENGNew: ``Echigoya, you are a wicked one indeed... Ho ho ho!!!``
changeReason: Old translates 越後屋 as 'Con man' while new preserves 'Echigoya'; consistent with the period drama role-play scene where Mion plays the corrupt magistrate addressing the merchant.

TextENGNew: ``Wh-What’s going on?! Keiichi-kun and Mii-chan are playing the evil magistrate...?@k@vS20/02/440200037. And the corrupt merchant?!``
changeReason: Old says 'evil bailiff' and 'con man' while new correctly says 'evil magistrate' and 'corrupt merchant'; these are the proper stock roles from Jidaigeki (period drama) that the characters are parodying.

TextENGNew: ``Curse you, Renanosuke Ryuugu! We meet at last, after a hundred years!@k@vS20/01/440100039. Now, prepare yourself~!!``
changeReason: Old mistranslates the idiom ここで会ったが百年目 ('we meet at last after a hundred years') as 'thou meetest thy maker' (threatening death); new correctly captures the dramatic confrontation idiom.

TextENGNew: ``Wah! Wah!@k@vS20/02/440200039. Suke-san, Kaku-san—teach them a lesson!!``
``Aye aye, ma’am!!!``
changeReason: Old says 'sir' while new says 'ma'am'; Satoko is responding to Rena (who is female), so 'ma'am' is the correct form of address.

TextENGNew: ``...I expect my payment deposited into my Swiss bank account.``
It had come to this! All that was left was an all-out brawl among the five of us...!!!
Right on cue, Rena’s devastating punch connected.
In the end, evil—meaning Mion and myself—was vanquished...
``...I really think Rena should go on a journey to set the world right. How about starting with parliament?``
``...That won’t work. She’d just swipe cute things along the way.``
changeReason: Old mistranslates Mion's line as 'I'll get kyute stuff for you on my travels' (as if Mion is offering to go) while new correctly says 'She'd just swipe cute things along the way' (Mion explaining why sending Rena on a justice journey would fail).

TextENGNew: The bell rang, signaling the end of break.
``Come on, come on, Keiichi-kun, Mii-chan! The teacher’s coming!``
I took Rena’s hand and got to my feet.
The teacher had just walked into the classroom.
...Just one more hour.@kGood grief.@k...Time to push through one more!
